content.text: Embedding models improve over time. A better model means better retrieval quality — but upgrading is not free. Every document in your index was embedded with the old model, and vectors from different models live in different geometric spaces. Querying a v2-embedded question against a v1-embedded index returns meaningless results silently: there is no error, just wrong answers. This document covers how to version embedding models, track which version is active, and run a zero-downtime migration across millions of vectors.

attrs.alt: Three-phase blue-green migration: Phase 1 (v1 live) — all reads and writes go to the v1 index. Phase 2 (dual-write + backfill) — new writes go to both v1 and v2, a background job backfills existing vectors into v2, all queries still served from v1. Phase 3 (cutover) — all reads and writes switch to v2, v1 kept 7 days for rollback then deleted. An embedding_config table tracks which model version is active. Warning box: query and document embeddings must use the same model version or similarity scores are meaningless.

content.text: Embedding models map text into a high-dimensional vector space. The geometry is model-specific: the vector for "connection pooling" produced by
is not comparable to the vector produced by

. Cosine similarity between vectors from different models is undefined — numerically it returns a value between -1 and 1, but it does not reflect semantic similarity. The system will return results without raising an error, which makes the failure silent and difficult to diagnose.

This means you must guarantee that when a user's query is embedded, it uses exactly the same model that was used to embed every document in the target index. Tracking this is the core problem of embedding versioning.
Store the active embedding model in a database table, not in a config file. Application code reads this at startup (and caches it) to know which model to call for both ingestion and query embedding.

CREATE TABLE embedding_config (
    id          SERIAL      PRIMARY KEY,
    model_name  TEXT        NOT NULL,
    provider    TEXT        NOT NULL,   -- 'cohere', 'openai', 'local', ...
    dimensions  INT         NOT NULL,
    version     TEXT        NOT NULL,   -- 'v1', 'v2', ...
    is_active   BOOLEAN     NOT NULL DEFAULT false,
    notes       TEXT,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

-- Only one row should be active at a time
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON embedding_config (is_active) WHERE is_active = true;

-- Seed with current model
INSERT INTO embedding_config (model_name, provider, dimensions, version, is_active, notes)
VALUES ('text-embedding-3-small', 'openai', 1536, 'v1', true,
        'Initial model — baseline for all existing chunks');

import functools
import psycopg2

@functools.lru_cache(maxsize=1)
def get_active_embedding_config(conn_str: str) -> dict:
    """Cache the active config at startup; invalidate on model upgrade."""
    conn = psycopg2.connect(conn_str)
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(
            "SELECT model_name, provider, dimensions, version "
            "FROM embedding_config WHERE is_active = true"
        )
        row = cur.fetchone()
        if not row:
            raise RuntimeError("No active embedding config found")
        return {"model": row[0], "provider": row[1], "dims": row[2], "version": row[3]}

# Usage everywhere in the application
config = get_active_embedding_config(DSN)
# Embed with config["model"] — never hardcode the model name in embed calls
on every chunk row. This lets you identify which chunks still need re-embedding during a migration and verify completeness afterward.

ALTER TABLE document_chunks
    ADD COLUMN embedding_version TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'v1';

-- Index to efficiently find chunks that need re-embedding
CREATE INDEX ON document_chunks (embedding_version)
    WHERE embedding_version != 'v2';  -- partial: only pending chunks
The safest migration runs in three phases: dual-write, backfill, then cutover. Reads stay on v1 until v2 is complete and verified.
Before touching existing chunks, update the ingest pipeline to write to both the v1 and v2 indexes simultaneously. New documents get both embeddings; existing documents still have only v1.

import cohere
import openai

oa = openai.OpenAI()
co = cohere.Client("cohere-key")

def embed_v1(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
    resp = oa.embeddings.create(input=texts, model="text-embedding-3-small")
    return [d.embedding for d in resp.data]

def embed_v2(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
    resp = co.embed(texts=texts, model="embed-english-v3.0",
                    input_type="search_document")
    return resp.embeddings

def ingest_dual_write(conn, doc_id: str, chunks: list[dict]) -> None:
    texts = [c["text"] for c in chunks]
    v1_embs = embed_v1(texts)
    v2_embs = embed_v2(texts)

    with conn, conn.cursor() as cur:
        for i, (chunk, e1, e2) in enumerate(zip(chunks, v1_embs, v2_embs)):
            cur.execute(
                """
                INSERT INTO document_chunks
                    (doc_id, chunk_index, chunk_text, embedding, embedding_v2, embedding_version)
                VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s, 'v2')
                """,
                (doc_id, i, chunk["text"], e1, e2),
            )
Run a background job that re-embeds old chunks in batches. Use
to find pending rows. Process in small batches with a brief sleep between to avoid overloading the embedding API.

import time, logging

BATCH = 96   # Cohere max batch size

def backfill_v2(conn) -> None:
    total = 0
    while True:
        with conn.cursor() as cur:
            cur.execute(
                """
                SELECT id, chunk_text
                FROM   document_chunks
                WHERE  embedding_version != 'v2'
                LIMIT  %s
                FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED
                """,
                (BATCH,),
            )
            rows = cur.fetchall()

        if not rows:
            logging.info("Backfill complete: %d chunks re-embedded", total)
            return

        ids   = [r[0] for r in rows]
        texts = [r[1] for r in rows]
        embs  = embed_v2(texts)

        with conn, conn.cursor() as cur:
            for row_id, emb in zip(ids, embs):
                cur.execute(
                    "UPDATE document_chunks "
                    "SET embedding_v2 = %s, embedding_version = 'v2' "
                    "WHERE id = %s",
                    (emb, row_id),
                )
        total += len(rows)
        logging.info("Backfilled %d chunks so far", total)
        time.sleep(0.1)   # rate-limit buffer
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM document_chunks WHERE embedding_version != 'v2'
for 7 days in case you need to roll back, then drop it:
ALTER TABLE document_chunks DROP COLUMN embedding_v1_retired
For managed vector databases that do not support multiple vector columns, use separate indexes (namespaces in Pinecone, separate collections in Qdrant):

# Pinecone blue-green via namespaces
import pinecone

pc = pinecone.Pinecone(api_key="your-key")
index = pc.Index("my-index")

# Phase 1-2: dual-write to both namespaces
def ingest_with_migration(chunks, v1_embs, v2_embs):
    index.upsert(vectors=[...], namespace="v1")  # existing namespace
    index.upsert(vectors=[...], namespace="v2")  # new namespace

# Phase 3: update ACTIVE_NAMESPACE env var / config, then delete v1 namespace
ACTIVE_NAMESPACE = "v2"   # flip this after backfill complete

def query(vector):
    return index.query(vector=vector, top_k=5,
                       namespace=ACTIVE_NAMESPACE, include_metadata=True)

# After 7 days: delete old namespace
# index.delete(delete_all=True, namespace="v1")
Never cut over based on backfill completion alone. Run a retrieval evaluation on a sample of 50–100 labelled queries before switching production traffic:

def evaluate_retrieval(queries_with_relevant_ids: list[dict], top_k: int = 5) -> dict:
    """
    queries_with_relevant_ids: [{"query": str, "relevant": set[str]}]
    Returns recall@k and MRR for v1 and v2.
    """
    v1_recall, v2_recall = [], []
    for item in queries_with_relevant_ids:
        q = item["query"]
        relevant = item["relevant"]

        v1_hits = set(r["id"] for r in search_v1(embed_v1([q])[0], top_k))
        v2_hits = set(r["id"] for r in search_v2(embed_v2([q])[0], top_k))

        v1_recall.append(len(v1_hits & relevant) / max(len(relevant), 1))
        v2_recall.append(len(v2_hits & relevant) / max(len(relevant), 1))

    return {
        "v1_recall_at_k": sum(v1_recall) / len(v1_recall),
        "v2_recall_at_k": sum(v2_recall) / len(v2_recall),
    }
Add embedding_version to every chunk at write time.
It is the source of truth for migration progress.
Invalidate the config cache on cutover.
, trigger a cache clear or restart workers so they pick up the new active model.
Monitor embedding API costs during backfill.
Re-embedding 10M chunks is a significant API call. Track cost and rate-limit headroom before starting.
so multiple backfill workers can run in parallel without double-processing.
). PostgreSQL requires a column drop and re-add; plan accordingly.
Do not drop the old data until v2 has served production traffic for at least a week with no regression.

Embedding versioning requires tracking the active model in a database table, stamping every chunk with its embedding version, and running a three-phase blue-green migration when upgrading: dual-write new documents to both versions, run a background backfill job for existing chunks, then atomically cut over reads and writes to the new index after evaluating retrieval quality. Never mix embedding model versions within a single index — the failure is silent and the results are meaningless.
